info.title: Internal Developer Portal's Go Template - Docket Photo/Document sharing API
info.description:
**Docket** is a photo/document sharing service used as the Go template for the **Internal Developer Portal**. Developers fork this template, replace the business logic with their own, and ship — the wiring for infrastructure (Postgres, MongoDB, NATS, Redis, MinIO), observability (OpenTelemetry tracing, Prometheus metrics, JSON logs), and API-key auth stays in place.

## Live services vs. in-memory fallback

Docket is designed to run **with or without** its backing services. On startup each backend is probed once with a 2-second timeout; if it doesn't answer, an in-memory Go implementation is used instead and a WARN is logged. `GET /healthz` reports the mode of every backend.

| Backend | Purpose | When live | When unreachable (fallback) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MinIO** (S3) | Raw file bytes | Uploaded bytes persist in the bucket | Bytes stored in a Go map — lost on restart |
| **MongoDB** | File metadata | Documents persist in `docket.files` collection | Stored in a Go map — lost on restart |
| **Postgres** | Audit log | Rows persist in `file_records` table | Stored in a Go slice — lost on restart |
| **NATS JetStream** | Upload events | Events published under `docket.files.*` subjects | Events kept in memory with no consumer |
| **Redis** | View counters | Counter keys under `docket:views:*` | In-memory `int64` — lost on restart |
| **Jaeger** (OTel) | Distributed tracing | Spans exported via OTLP | Tracing silently disabled |

> **The event bus is wired for demonstration only** — the built-in NATS consumer just logs received events. Real downstream consumers (thumbnailer, indexer, notifier) are what you'd add when forking this template.

## Authentication

Write endpoints (`POST`, `DELETE`) check the header `X-API-Key: <your key>` against the value of the `DOCKET_API_KEY` env var. Only requests carrying the matching value are accepted. If the env var is empty, the check is **skipped** and the API runs in open mode — useful for local dev, **unsafe** anywhere else. Swagger UI is pre-authorized with the dev key `dev-secret-change-me` so *Try it out* works instantly.

## Optional backing service UIs

Each backing service ships its own web console on a known port. If your stack is running, they're reachable at `<BASE_URL>:<port>` — the exact host depends on where the stack is deployed. If the services aren't running, Docket runs in in-memory mode and there are no UIs to browse.

tags.name: Files
tags.description: Upload, fetch, list, download, and delete files.

tags.name: Audit
tags.description: Read-only audit trail backed by Postgres.

tags.name: Operations
tags.description: Health, metrics, seed, and load-test utilities.

get.description:
Live application metrics in Prometheus text format. **This endpoint always returns data even if Prometheus itself is not running** — the endpoint is just a URL that prints numbers on demand. To actually collect and visualize the numbers over time you need a scraper (Prometheus server, Grafana Cloud, VictoriaMetrics, OpenTelemetry Collector) that hits this URL on a schedule.

Two categories of data are returned:

**Go runtime metrics** (auto-collected — always present):
- `go_gc_duration_seconds` — how long garbage collection pauses have been
- `go_goroutines` — number of concurrent goroutines running right now
- `go_memstats_*` — heap size, allocations, memory usage
- `go_threads` — OS thread count

These help spot memory leaks and runaway goroutines.

**Application counters** (defined in `internal/metrics/metrics.go`):
- `docket_http_requests_total{route,method,status}` — HTTP request counts
- `docket_http_request_duration_seconds` — histogram of request durations
- `docket_uploads_total{mode}` — uploads partitioned by `live` vs `memory` mode
- `docket_events_published_total{topic}` — NATS publishes
- `docket_cache_ops_total{op,result}` — cache operations

Returns recent file **metadata** documents, newest first. Reads from **MongoDB (or in-memory fallback)**.

**Does NOT return file bytes** — call `GET /files/{id}/download` for the bytes.

post.summary: Upload a file — exercises ALL backends
post.description:
Uploads a single file via `multipart/form-data`. **This is the showcase endpoint — it touches every backend in one request.**

**What happens internally** — each step goes to the live backend if that service is connected; otherwise the data is stored in an in-memory Go structure that vanishes on restart:

| Step | Live backend | In-memory fallback |
|---|---|---|
| 1. File bytes | MinIO (S3 object) | Go map keyed by ID |
| 2. Metadata document | MongoDB `docket.files` | Go map keyed by ID |
| 3. Audit row (`action='upload'`) | Postgres `file_records` | Go slice |
| 4. `file.uploaded` event | NATS subject `docket.files.uploaded` | Go channel (no consumer) |
| 5. View counter | Redis (initialized on first read) | Go `int64` |

Check `/healthz` at any time to see which backends are `live` and which are `memory`.

**Tracing**: if `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT` is configured, one upload produces one trace with a span per backend touched. Open your tracing UI (Jaeger, Grafana Tempo, SigNoz) to see the flame graph.

**Auth**: `X-API-Key` header required.

201.description: Created — returns the persisted metadata including the generated file ID.

get.description: Returns the metadata document for one file and atomically increments its view counter. Metadata lookup goes to **MongoDB (or in-memory fallback)**; counter increment goes to **Redis (or in-memory fallback)**. Every call bumps the `docket:views:<id>` key by one.

delete.summary: Delete a file from every backend
delete.description:
Removes a file from every backend at once.

**What happens internally** — each step goes to the live backend if connected, otherwise updates the in-memory equivalent:

| Step | Live backend | In-memory fallback |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Object deleted | MinIO | Removed from Go map |
| 2. Metadata removed | MongoDB | Removed from Go map |
| 3. Audit row (`action='delete'`) | Postgres `file_records` (kept forever) | Appended to Go slice |
| 4. `file.deleted` event | NATS subject `docket.files.deleted` | Go channel (no consumer) |

**Auth**: `X-API-Key` header required.

get.description:
Streams the raw bytes of a file. Reads from **MinIO (or in-memory blob map fallback)**. The response `Content-Type` matches what was set at upload time, and `Content-Disposition` includes the original filename so browsers save it correctly.

**Note**: this returns binary — Swagger UI will show gibberish in the response body. Use the **Download file** link Swagger renders below the response, or open the URL directly in a new tab.

parameters.name: inline
parameters.description: If true, sets `Content-Disposition: inline` so the browser renders the file (PDF viewer, image preview) instead of saving it.

get.summary: Audit trail for a single file
get.description: Returns every recorded action (upload, view, delete) for the given file, newest first. Reads from **Postgres `file_records` (or in-memory slice fallback)**. Useful for compliance and debugging.

post.summary: Insert N demo files (for empty systems)
post.description:
**What it does**: Creates N fake files (tiny text files with content like 'seed-file-3') so your app isn't empty. Handy right after startup so `/files` returns results, load tests have data to hit, and the backend web UIs aren't blank.

**When the backends are running** (Postgres + MongoDB + MinIO connected):
- File bytes → saved to MinIO under a random UUID
- Metadata → inserted into MongoDB
- Audit record → inserted into Postgres `file_records` table
- NATS event → **not** published (seeding is bookkeeping, not real activity)

**When the backends are NOT running**:
- Same fake files are created, but everything lives in a Go map inside the running process
- You can still hit `/files`, download them, run load tests against them
- When the app is stopped, everything vanishes

**Limit**: capped at 1000 files per call so you don't accidentally flood the system.

**Auth**: `X-API-Key` header required.

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: How many files to create (default 20, max 1000).

post.description:
**What it does**: Simulates traffic against your running app to see how fast it can serve reads.

**The two knobs**:
- **`n`** — total number of operations to perform (max 10,000).
- **`concurrency`** — how many run *at the same time* (default 50).

**Analogy**: `n=10000, concurrency=50` is like having 10,000 customers in line with 50 cashiers. Each cashier serves one customer at a time; when a cashier finishes, the next customer steps up. Higher concurrency = more parallel pressure on the system.

**What each operation does**: picks a random existing file, looks up its metadata (from **MongoDB or in-memory fallback**), and increments its view counter (in **Redis or in-memory fallback**). Two tiny reads per op.

**Requirement**: at least one file must exist — call `/seed?n=20` first if the system is empty, or you'll get `400`.

**Response** — total time, requests-per-second, and latency percentiles:
- `p50_ms` = the middle request (half were faster, half slower)
- `p95_ms` = only 5% of requests took longer than this
- `p99_ms` = only 1% took longer — good for spotting tail-latency issues
- `max_ms` = the slowest single request

**While it runs**: watch `/metrics` — the `docket_http_requests_total` counter jumps. Watch your tracing UI (if configured) — each op generates a trace.

**Auth**: `X-API-Key` header required.

parameters.name: concurrency
parameters.description: How many run in parallel at once.

200.description: Percentile breakdown: requests, total_ms, rps, p50_ms, p90_ms, p95_ms, p99_ms, max_ms.

400.description: No files exist yet — call /seed first.

apiKey.name: X-API-Key
apiKey.description: Every write endpoint (`POST`, `DELETE`) checks the `X-API-Key` header before letting the request through. The server compares the header value against the `DOCKET_API_KEY` env var it was started with — only calls carrying the matching value are accepted, otherwise `401 Unauthorized`. **If the env var is empty, the check is skipped and anyone on the network can write to the API** — a startup WARN is logged. Set a real value for anything beyond local dev. Swagger UI is pre-authorized with `dev-secret-change-me` so *Try it out* works instantly.
